title: GitHub stale action
body:
Add a GitHub action to mark issues and PRs as stale and 
to eventually close them after a grace period.

Signed-off-by: Valentin Rothberg <rothberg@redhat.com>

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *vrothberg* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Flibpod). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/libpod/blob/master/OWNERS)~~ [vrothberg] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment
LGTM, although 30 days might be a little quick.  Will this wait 30 days from today to start removing, or will it remove issues/PRs that have not been updated from Sep28?

The code looks fine, but I'm not at all a fan of this.  I fear it will cause more work for us than necessary, and angst for our community.  I'm guessing we'll get the warning then jump in and change the issue to "do-not-close" 90% or more of the time and I don't see the payoff there.  But that's my $.02

> LGTM, although 30 days might be a little quick. Will this wait 30 days from today to start removing, or will it remove issues/PRs that have not been updated from Sep28?

I agree, 30 days is too aggressive.  We can try with 90 days first and see how it goes.

>  LGTM, although 30 days might be a little quick. 

We discussed 30 days yesterday in the meeting. @baude wdyt?

> Will this wait 30 days from today to start removing, or will it remove issues/PRs that have not been updated from Sep28?

I expect it to count from Sep 28 in this case. Note that the bot will not close issues and PRs after 30 days but it will drop a comment and close after a grace period of 7 days and only close in the absence of activity or without the do-not-close label.



> The code looks fine, but I'm not at all a fan of this. I fear it will cause more work for us than necessary, and angst for our community. I'm guessing we'll get the warning then jump in and change the issue to "do-not-close" 90% or more of the time and I don't see the payoff there. But that's my $.02

I understand this concern but accumulating old PRs and issues isn't good either. At the very least, the bot will buy us time going through the growing list and sending reminders. Certainly, the "do-not-close" label must be used with care. If it's overused we can remove it (also from the bot).

I will merge this and then we can play with the number after the fact.  This will at least get us to look at old stuff every 30 days and force people to acknowledge they still want it, or we close them.  Rather then letting them rot.

/lgtm
